messages.content: You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. Programs may use (module name ...) wrapping or top-level script mode. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string, #0 is the first as an int. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, counter, sort_by, entries, enumerate, scan, map_kv, map_pairs, find_all, regex_find, regex_match, regex_replace. Prefer functional pipelines over for-each+accumulator. Empty collections: [] and {}. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Take a string in arg0. Print all http:// and https:// URLs (run of non-whitespace characters), one per line in order.
(for-each u (find_all "https?://\S+" $0) (println u))

Take a multi-line text in arg0. Print all ISO-format dates (YYYY-MM-DD) found, one per line in order.

Take a string in arg0. Print 'yes' if it is a syntactically valid dotted-quad IPv4 (four 0-255 integers separated by dots, no leading zeros except '0' itself), else 'no'.

(set parts (split $0 "."))
(set ok (eq (len parts) 4))
(if ok
  (for-each p parts
    (if (not (regex_match "^(0|[1-9]\d{0,2})$" p))
      (set ok false)
      (if (gt (parse_int p) 255) (set ok false) (set _ 0)))))
(println (if ok "yes" "no"))

Take a Python source code string in arg0. Print the names of all top-level (no leading whitespace) `def NAME(` definitions, one per line in order.

(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set m (regex_find "^def \w+\(" line))
  (if (ne m "") (println (string_slice m 4 (sub (len m) 5)))))

Take a multi-line text in arg0 and a substring in arg1. Print every occurrence of the substring (one per line) across all lines, preserving order.

(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set n (sub (len (split line $1)) 1))
  (for i 0 n (println $1)))
